common.desktopOnlyText:
Alguns caminhos exigem uma visão mais ampla.
Volte em uma tela maior.
common.desktopOnlyTextIndex:
Alguns caminhos exigem uma visão mais ampla.
Volte em uma tela maior.

index.zen:
Um sistema funcionou por mil dias sem erro.
No milésimo primeiro dia, ele falhou.

O desenvolvedor perguntou: “Quando isso começou?”
Mas o sistema nunca havia aprendido a falar.
index.observeHint: O que muda quando você pode ver?
index.insight: Agora o sistema fala. Agora você pode ouvir.

koan01.title: The Silent Failure
koan01.zen:
Two systems run the same code.
One fails in silence. The other fails and tells you why.
koan01.scenarioBanner: A user reports: “Checkout is broken. I can’t place an order.”
koan01.instruction: Click each system to investigate the failure.
koan01.q1: Which system can you actually debug?
koan01.q1Correct: System B gives you data to work with. System A gives you nothing.
koan01.q1Wrong: System A gave you nothing when you clicked it. You can’t debug what you can’t see.
koan01.q2:
System B emits data about itself. System A does not.
What is the data that System B emits called?
koan01.q2WrongDebug: Debugging output is something a developer reads during development. This data is emitted continuously by the running system itself, in production, all the time.
koan01.q2WrongLogs: Error logs are one type of this data, but System B also showed latency numbers and request traces. The answer is broader than just logs.
koan01.insightP1: Data a system emits about itself.
koan01.insightP2: Without it, a system is a black box. With it, you can see inside.

koan02.title: Three Signals
koan02.zen:
A system speaks, but not with one voice.
It measures. It narrates. It records.
Three ways of seeing. Each reveals what the others cannot.
koan02.sortInstruction: A stream of telemetry arrives. Sort these items into three groups.
koan02.bucketEmptyA: click an item above, then choose A
koan02.bucketEmptyB: click an item above, then choose B
koan02.bucketEmptyC: click an item above, then choose C
koan02.sortFeedbackAllAssigned: Sort all six items into the three buckets first.
koan02.sortFeedbackEqualSize: Each bucket should have exactly two items.
koan02.sortHint1: Not quite. Look at the format of each item.
koan02.sortHint2: Hint: some items are numbers, some are paths between services, some are timestamped events.
koan02.sortHint3Fallback: Each bucket should contain items of the same kind. Numbers together, paths together, events together.
koan02.itemChar0: This item is a numeric measurement
koan02.itemChar1: This item shows a path between services
koan02.itemChar2: This item is a timestamped event record
koan02.itemChar3: This item is a numeric measurement
koan02.itemChar4: This item shows a path between services
koan02.itemChar5: This item is a timestamped event record
koan02.sortCorrectFeedback: Three kinds of data. Three ways to understand a system.
koan02.matchInstruction: Apply what you discovered. Which signal would you reach for in each scenario?
koan02.matchQ1: Your checkout is slow and getting worse over time. You need to see the trend.
koan02.matchQ2: A single request failed. You need to follow it through 4 services to find where it broke.
koan02.matchQ3: The database returned an error. You need the exact error message and timestamp.
koan02.matchFeedbackEmpty: Choose an answer for each question.
koan02.matchCorrect: Three signals. Three questions only they can answer.
koan02.matchWrong: Not quite. Think about what kind of data each scenario needs: a number over time, a path through services, or a discrete event.
koan02.insightLine1: Metrics measure. Traces narrate. Logs record.
koan02.insightLine2: Together, they are the three signals of observability.

koan03.pageTitle: Koan 3: What is a Metric? - OpenTelemetry Koans
koan03.title: Too Many to Hold
koan03.zen:
The system speaks. But a thousand events pour out every second.
Too many to hold. But if you count them, a pattern appears.
koan03.challengeText: The system is getting slow. Is it getting worse?
koan03.challengeSub: Can you tell from this stream of events?
koan03.chartHeader: what you built from the raw events
koan03.q1: What just happened when you clicked that button?
koan03.q1OptB: The events were counted and measured over time intervals
koan03.q1WrongA: The events are still there. You didn't delete anything. Look at what appeared in their place.
koan03.q1WrongC: The system didn't change. You changed how you're looking at it, from individual events to aggregated counts.
koan03.q2: What you just built, a number that measures something over time, has a name. What is it called?
koan03.q2WrongA: A log captures individual events, but you just collapsed events into a summary number.
koan03.q2WrongC: A trace follows a single request through a system, but you just aggregated many requests into a count.
koan03.insightLine1: A metric is a measurement captured over time.
koan03.insightLine2: It tells you how much, how fast, how often: the vital signs of a system.
koan03.insightLine3: You didn't just learn what a metric is. You built one.

koan04.pageTitle: Koan 4: Kinds of Metrics - OpenTelemetry Koans
koan04.title: Kinds of Metrics
koan04.zen:
Not all numbers behave the same way.
Watch closely. The differences matter.
koan04.statusDone: Three metrics, three behaviors. Can you name them?
koan04.labelTitle: Match each metric to its type
koan04.labelWrong: Not quite. Look at the hints below each chart.
koan04.labelEmpty: Select a type for each chart first.
koan04.hintA1: Total requests is a running total. What type tracks cumulative counts?
koan04.hintA2: Each new request adds to the total. This metric accumulates over time.
koan04.hintB1: Active connections can rise and fall. What type tracks a current level?
koan04.hintB2: This value reflects a snapshot of right now — not a total, not a distribution.
koan04.hintC1: Chart C doesn't show a line over time. What does it show instead?
koan04.hintC2: These bars show how values are distributed across ranges.
koan04.finalQ: You need to know if most requests are fast or if there are slow outliers. Which metric type?
koan04.finalQCorrect: A histogram shows how values are distributed. Most fast, some slow. You see the shape.
koan04.finalQWrong: Which chart shows you the spread of values across ranges?
koan04.insightLine1: Counter tracks a running total. Gauge measures a current level. Histogram reveals the shape.
koan04.insightLine2: Choose the type that matches the question you need to answer.

koan05.pageTitle: Koan 5: What is a Trace? - OpenTelemetry Koans
koan05.title: What is a Trace?
koan05.zen:
The metric said something was slow.
The logs said something went wrong.
But neither could say where, or in what order.
koan05.scenarioAlert: Users report that checkout is slow.
koan05.scenarioDetail: You have metrics and logs from four services: gateway, api, orders, and database. Can you find the bottleneck?
koan05.metricVerdict: Request count looks normal. But latency is high. The metric tells you something is slow, but not which service.
koan05.q1: From the metrics and logs alone, can you tell which service is causing the slowness?
koan05.q1OptC: No - I can’t see how the request flowed through the services
koan05.q1Correct: Right. The logs show activity from multiple services, but not how one request flowed through them.
koan05.q1WrongDb: The database appears in the logs, but so do three other services. Can you be sure it is the cause?
koan05.q1WrongGw: The gateway appears in the logs, but so do three other services. Can you be sure it is the cause?
koan05.statusNeedFollow: You need to follow a single request through all four services.
koan05.statusAfterFollow: Now you can see where the time went.
koan05.q2: What you just did, following one request through multiple services, is called a...
koan05.q2WrongMetric: Metrics measure aggregate numbers, not individual request paths.
koan05.q2WrongLog: Logs capture events at one point. You followed the request across all four services.
koan05.insightLine1: A trace follows a single request from beginning to end.
koan05.insightLine2: What metrics and logs could not reveal alone, the trace shows.

koan06.pageTitle: Koan 6: Steps in a Trace - OpenTelemetry Koans
koan06.title: The Steps Within
koan06.zen:
The trace showed the path. But the path has steps.
Each step has a story.
koan06.statusLoaded: Trace loaded. Study the bars above.
koan06.q1: This trace crossed four services. Look at the bars above. How many individual operations can you count?
koan06.q1Correct: Four bars, four operations: gateway, api, orders, database.
koan06.q1Wrong: Count each colored bar in the waterfall. Each bar is one operation.
koan06.mid1: Each of those bars represents one step: one unit of work within the trace.
koan06.q2: The database bar took 180ms out of the total 210ms. What does this tell you?
koan06.q2Correct: The database operation consumed 86% of the total time. That is where you would investigate.
koan06.q2Wrong: Look at the durations. The database bar is 180ms and the entire trace is 210ms.
koan06.mid2: By breaking a trace into its individual steps, you can see exactly where time is spent.
koan06.q3: Each of these individual steps in a trace has a name in OpenTelemetry. What is it called?
koan06.q3Correct: In OpenTelemetry, each step in a trace is called a span.
koan06.q3Wrong: Close, but OpenTelemetry uses a specific term for each step in a trace.
koan06.insightP1: A span is one step in a trace. Each span has a start, an end, and tells you what happened during that step.
koan06.insightP2: The waterfall shows how spans nest inside each other, revealing where time is spent.

koan07.pageTitle: Koan 7: The Missing Detail - OpenTelemetry Koans
koan07.title: The Missing Detail
koan07.zen:
The trace showed where. But what happened in that moment?
Something must have captured the details.
koan07.narrator1: The trace shows an error in the database span at 10:42:17. It took 180ms and failed. But what actually went wrong? What error message? What data was involved?
koan07.q1: From this trace, can you determine the specific error that caused the failure?
koan07.q1OptA: Yes, the ERROR status tells me what went wrong
koan07.q1OptB: No — I can see it failed, but not why
koan07.q1Correct: You can see the database span failed at 10:42:17 — but not why. The trace shows where and when. Something else captured the details.
koan07.q1Wrong: Look again — the span says ERROR, but does it say which error? A timeout? A connection refused? A constraint violation?
koan07.narrator2: Something did capture the exact moment of failure. Look at what appeared.
koan07.evidenceHeader: captured at the moment of failure
koan07.q2: Look at what just appeared. What did it capture?
koan07.q2OptB: The path of a request through services
koan07.q2OptC: A single event: what happened at one specific moment
koan07.q2Correct: Not a trend over time (that would be a metric). Not a path through services (that would be a trace). A single captured event.
koan07.q2Wrong: Think about what you see: a timestamp, a severity, a message, context. Is that a trend? A path? Or a moment?
koan07.q3: This kind of record, capturing a discrete event with severity and context, is called a...
koan07.q3Correct: A log record: a discrete event, captured with timestamp, severity, message, and context.
koan07.q3Wrong: A metric summarizes numbers. A span tracks one step in a path. This is something else entirely.
koan07.insightP1: Metrics measure trends. Traces follow paths. Log records capture the moments that matter.
koan07.insightP2: Each signal answers a different question. Together, they tell the full story.

koan08.title: Attributes
koan08.zen:
A number without a name is noise.
A span without detail is a blank page.
Attributes give telemetry its meaning: the who, what, where, and why.
koan08.bareFrustration: A developer sees this metric in a dashboard. They ask: Which endpoint was hit? What HTTP method? Did the request succeed? The metric alone cannot answer any of these questions.
koan08.challengeIntro: Choose the attribute that answers each question
koan08.statusEmpty: Choose an attribute for each question first.
koan08.statusWrong: Not quite. Read the hints below each wrong answer.
koan08.statusCorrect: Now the metric can answer every question.
koan08.hint1L1: The keyword is "endpoint": which attribute describes a route or path?
koan08.hint1L2: It starts with "http." and describes which URL path was called.
koan08.hint1L3: The answer is the attribute that maps to a URL route: http.route
koan08.hint2L1: Focus on "HTTP method": which attribute contains a request method?
koan08.hint2L2: Look for an attribute with both "http" and "method" in its name.
koan08.hint2L3: The attribute is http.request.method. It stores GET, POST, etc.
koan08.hint3L1: The question asks if the request "succeeded": what tells you success or failure?
koan08.hint3L2: Success means a status code like 200. Which attribute carries a status code?
koan08.hint3L3: The answer is http.response.status_code. It tells you if the request succeeded.
koan08.otherSignalsTitle: Attributes work on every signal, not just metrics
koan08.insightP1: Attributes are key-value pairs. They turn raw data into answers.
koan08.insightP2: Every metric, span, and log can carry them.
koan08.insightP3: Metrics have long called these dimensions, same truth, older name.

koan09.title: Resources
koan09.zen:
Three alarms fire at once.
Three identical errors.
But where did they come from?
koan09.ambiguityPrompt:
Three identical errors. But are they from the same service? Different services?
Different environments? You can’t tell.
koan09.q1OptB: Which service, version, and environment produced each one
koan09.q1Correct: Exactly. You need to know the source, not just the data.
koan09.q1Wrong: That would be an attribute, not the identity of the source itself.
koan09.q2: Now you can see the resources. How many services are affected?
koan09.q2Hint1: Look at the service.name field on each card.
koan09.q2Hint2: Two cards say "orders" and one says "payment", so how many distinct names is that?
koan09.q2Hint3: There are exactly 2 unique service names: orders and payment.
koan09.q3: Is this happening in production only?
koan09.q3Hint1: Check the deployment.environment field: is it the same on all three?
koan09.q3Hint2: One card shows "staging" while two show "production", so it is not production only.
koan09.q3Hint3: The second card has deployment.environment = "staging", so the answer is No.
koan09.crossSignalLabel: Resources attach to all signals, not just metrics
koan09.insightP1: A resource is the identity card of a telemetry source.
koan09.insightP2: Attributes describe what happened. Resources tell you who reported it.

koan10.title: Naming Things
koan10.zen:
Attributes let you attach any key to your telemetry.
But what happens when everyone picks their own?
koan10.scenario:
Your HTTP handler just returned a response. You want to record the status code as an attribute.
What will you name the key?
koan10.comparisonMatched: You and Team {team} agreed. But the others didn’t. Four developers, three different names.
koan10.comparisonUnmatched: Four developers. Four names. All mean the same thing.
koan10.taskPrompt:
An incident: 500 errors are spiking across all three services.
You need to find every request that returned a 500. Try querying for them.
koan10.result1Note: Only 1 of 3 services found. Team B calls it response_status. Team C calls it http_code.
koan10.result2Note: Query 2 of 3. Still missing Team C.
koan10.result3Note: Three queries to answer one question. During an outage, this costs minutes you don’t have.
koan10.frustration: 3 queries for 1 answer. Every name is different. Every type is different.
koan10.q1:
Each developer independently chose a different name for the same thing.
Why does this make querying so hard?
koan10.q1OptB: No single query can match all three names at once
koan10.q1Correct: Exactly. Three names, zero consistency — no single query can find them all.
koan10.q1Scaffold1: Look at the query results. The data is there, the names just don’t match.
koan10.q1Scaffold2: Each team picked their own key name. Your query can only use one name at a time.
koan10.q1Scaffold3: Three different attribute names means three separate queries. A single query can’t match all three.
koan10.unifiedHeader: one name, one query, all services
koan10.unifiedNote: One query. All three services. Same name, same type, same meaning.
koan10.q2:
Team D joins. They need to record the HTTP request method.
Following the same naming pattern (dotted namespaces), what should they call it?
koan10.q2Correct: Dotted namespaces: http.request.method. Consistent, predictable, universal.
koan10.q2Wrong: Look at the pattern: http.response.status_code. The method would follow the same structure.
koan10.insightP1: These shared naming rules are called semantic conventions.
koan10.insightP2: When everyone speaks the same language, one query finds everything.

koan11.pageTitle: Koan 11: Making Code Speak - OpenTelemetry Koans
koan11.title: Silent Code
koan11.zen:
The code runs. It works.
But it says nothing about how it works.
Someone must teach it to speak.
koan11.problemText: This function handles checkout in production. A customer reports their checkout took 30 seconds. You look for data, timing, order details, anything, but there is none. The code works, but it tells you nothing.
koan11.statusInitial: Add each line in order, starting from the top of the function.
koan11.fbStartSpan: A span begins. The function now has a way to record its timing.
koan11.fbSetAttribute: The span now carries context: which order is being processed.
koan11.fbMeterRecord: A metric is recorded. You can now track order totals over time.
koan11.fbSpanEnd: The span is closed. Duration is captured. The function now speaks.
koan11.fbConsole: console.log writes text to stdout, but it is not structured telemetry. The Collector cannot process it.
koan11.fbWrongOrder: That line belongs further down. Look for what goes in the highlighted slot first.
koan11.statusComplete: The code is no longer silent.
koan11.q1: You just made silent code emit structured telemetry. This act, adding code that makes a program produce telemetry, is called...
koan11.q1WrongA: Debugging is finding and fixing errors. What you did was add new code to produce telemetry, not fix a bug.
koan11.q1WrongC: Compiling translates source code to machine code. You added telemetry-producing code to the function.
koan11.q1WrongFallback: Not quite. Think about what you just did to the code.
koan11.q1Correct: Instrumentation: the act of adding code that makes a program produce telemetry.
koan11.insightLine1: Instrumentation is the bridge between code and observability.
koan11.insightLine2: Without it, code runs in the dark. With it, every function can describe what it did, how long it took, and what it touched.

koan12.pageTitle: Koan 12: What Gets Exported - OpenTelemetry Koans
koan12.title: What Gets Exported
koan12.zen:
The code speaks. But in what language?
Instrumentation creates telemetry inside the program.
To leave the program, it must become structured data.
Read the code. Predict the output.
koan12.sceneHeader: read the code, fill in the blanks
koan12.hintRow: read the code on the left → fill the dropdowns on the right
koan12.statusFillAll: Fill in all three blanks first.
koan12.statusWrongCount: {count} wrong. Check the highlighted code on the left.
koan12.statusAllCorrect: All correct. You constructed the exported data.
koan12.hintName1: Look at the string passed to tracer.startSpan().
koan12.hintName2: The span name is the argument to startSpan(). What string is in those parentheses?
koan12.hintAttr1L1: Look at the first setAttribute() call. What is the key?
koan12.hintAttr1L2: setAttribute("order.id", "8842"): the first argument is the attribute key.
koan12.hintAttr1L3: The key is "order.id". It is the first argument to setAttribute().
koan12.hintAttr2L1: Look at the second setAttribute() call. What key is used?
koan12.hintAttr2L2: setAttribute("http.request.method", "POST"): the attribute key matches the first string argument.
koan12.hintAttr2L3: The key is "http.request.method". It maps directly from the code.
koan12.callout: This structured format is called OTLP, OpenTelemetry Protocol. The code created telemetry. OTLP is how it leaves the program as structured data.
koan12.q1: Why does telemetry need a standard format like OTLP?
koan12.q1OptB: So any compatible tool can receive and understand it
koan12.q1OptC: Because JSON is required by law
koan12.q1Correct: A standard format means any compatible backend can receive, store, and analyze the data.
koan12.q1Wrong: Think about interoperability: what happens when many different tools need to read the same data?
koan12.insightLine1: You read the code and constructed the output yourself.
koan12.insightLine2: Instrumentation creates telemetry. OTLP gives it a universal shape, so any tool can understand it.

koan13.title: Data Formats
koan13.zen:
The message is the same. Only the language changes.
A system that speaks one language reaches one listener.
A system that speaks many reaches all.
koan13.logLine1: Sending span checkout to metrics backend...
koan13.logError1: Export failed: unsupported format. Cannot parse binary encoding.
koan13.q1: The exporter sent valid telemetry, but the backend rejected it. Why?
koan13.q1OptCorrect: The data was in a format the backend doesn’t understand
koan13.q1OptNoSpans: The backend can’t receive spans at all
koan13.q1Correct: Right. The data was valid, but the backend couldn’t read its format.
koan13.q1Hint1: Re-read the error. It says "unsupported format." What does that tell you about the data itself?
koan13.q1Hint2: The data wasn’t broken or invalid. The backend just doesn’t speak that language.
koan13.formatBDesc2: Same data as Format A, packed into
koan13.backend2Desc: Reads plain text, one key=value pair per line
koan13.backend3Desc: Accepts structured JSON with named fields
koan13.hintBinary1: Look at the format cards. Which one shows data as non-human-readable blocks?
koan13.hintBinary2: Format B shows binary-encoded hex blocks. That matches "compact binary-encoded data."
koan13.hintText1: Look at the format cards. Which one shows key=value pairs, one per line?
koan13.hintText2: Format C uses key=value pairs, one field per line.
koan13.hintJson1: Look at the format cards. Which one uses curly braces and named fields?
koan13.hintJson2: Format A is JSON with curly braces, keys in quotes, and nested values.
koan13.revealIntro: In practice, these backends have names you’ll encounter:
koan13.revealJaeger: A tracing tool that accepts binary-encoded data
koan13.revealPrometheus: A metrics system that scrapes key=value text
koan13.revealElastic: Logging tools that ingest structured JSON
koan13.insightLine1: The data is the same. The format is the envelope.

koan14.title: The Hub
koan14.zen: Many rivers flow. They do not each find the sea alone.
koan14.q1Context: Each service sends telemetry to every backend. With 3 services and 3 backends, that is 9 connections.
koan14.q1: You add a 4th service. How many total connections do you need now?
koan14.q1Correct: 4 services × 3 backends = 12 connections. Every service must know every backend.
koan14.q1Wrong10: Each of the 4 services needs a connection to each of the 3 backends. Multiply, don’t add.
koan14.q1Wrong15: 15 would be 5×3. We only have 4 services and 3 backends.
koan14.narr1Line1: Every new service adds 3 connections. Every new backend adds N more. The total is always services × backends.
koan14.narr1Line2: What if there was a single point in the middle?
koan14.q2Context: A hub appears between the services and backends. Services send to the hub. The hub sends to backends.
koan14.q2OptCorrect: It reduced N×M connections to N+M
koan14.q2Correct: 4 + 3 = 7 connections instead of 12. With 10 services and 5 backends: 15 instead of 50.
koan14.q2WrongDeleted: The hub forwards all telemetry. Nothing is deleted.
koan14.q2WrongFaster: The hub adds a hop, so latency doesn’t improve. Think about connections.
koan14.narr2Line1: This hub is called the OpenTelemetry Collector.
koan14.narr2Line2: It has three internal stages. Based on its position in the diagram, between services and backends, what do you think each stage does?
koan14.q3: Match each stage to what it does:
koan14.q3AllCorrect: Receive → Process → Export. The three stages of the Collector pipeline.
koan14.q3WrongTemplate: Think about what "{stageName}" means in context.
koan14.insightLine1: The Collector is the hub. Services send to it. It processes and forwards to backends.

koan15.title: The Pipeline
koan15.zen:
A river without banks is a flood.
The pipeline gives telemetry its path:
where to enter, how to flow, where to arrive.
koan15.statusFillAll: Fill in all three pipeline fields first.
koan15.statusWrongFields: {count} field(s) incorrect. See the highlighted YAML sections.
koan15.hintRecInline: Look at the receivers: section above.
koan15.hintRec1: Look at lines 1-4. What receiver is defined under "receivers:"?
koan15.hintRec2: The receivers section defines "otlp:". The pipeline must use a receiver that exists above.
koan15.hintRec3: The only receiver defined is otlp (lines 1-4). Choose otlp.
koan15.hintProcInline: Look at the processors: section above.
koan15.hintProc1: Look at lines 6-7. What processor is defined under "processors:"?
koan15.hintProc2: The processors section defines "batch:". The pipeline must reference it by name.
koan15.hintProc3: The only processor defined is batch (lines 6-7). Choose batch.
koan15.hintExpInline: Look at the exporters: section above.
koan15.hintExp1: Look at lines 9-11. What exporter is defined under "exporters:"?
koan15.hintExp2: The exporters section defines "otlp:" with endpoint "jaeger:4317". Pick the matching name.
koan15.hintExp3: The only exporter defined is otlp (lines 9-11). Choose otlp.
koan15.q1: The pipeline connects receivers to processors to exporters. What does this mean?
koan15.q1OptA: Data flows in a fixed order: receive, process, export
koan15.q1OptB: All three run at the same time randomly
koan15.q1OptC: You only need one of the three
koan15.q1Correct: Exactly. The pipeline enforces a clear sequence: data enters, gets processed, then leaves.
koan15.q1Wrong: Not quite. Think about the arrows in the diagram: they go in one direction.
koan15.insightLine1: The pipeline is the Collector’s blueprint.
koan15.insightLine2: YAML tells it: where to listen, how to process, where to send.

koan16.title: Sampling
koan16.zen:
To see everything is to see nothing.
The master does not watch every raindrop.
They watch the ones that change the river’s course.
koan16.instruction: In the pipeline you just configured, a processor sits between receiving and exporting. Sampling is one such processor. It decides what to keep.
koan16.explanationRandom: ~{kept}/20 traces kept. {errKept} of {errTotal} error traces survived.
koan16.explanationSmart: {kept}/20 traces kept. All slow (>500 ms) and error traces included.
koan16.strategySmart: Keep only slow or error traces
koan16.q1: Which sampling strategy kept the most useful data with the least volume?
koan16.q1OptSmart: Keep only slow or error traces
koan16.q1Correct: Tail-based sampling keeps what matters. But head-based is simpler, lower overhead, and gives an unbiased view of all traffic. Most systems use both.
koan16.q1Wrong1: Compare the three strategies. Which kept the error and slow traces while storing the fewest total?
koan16.q1Wrong2: Look at what the ‘smart’ strategy kept vs. what ‘random’ dropped.
koan16.insightLine1: At low traffic you can keep everything. At scale, you can’t.
koan16.insightLine2: Sampling is the art of choosing what to keep when you can’t keep it all.

koan17.title: Service Maps
koan17.zen:
No one drew the map.
The requests drew it themselves.
each call a line, each service a point,
until the shape of the system appeared.
koan17.q1: You have 6 services but no architecture diagram. You do have trace data from production. Can you figure out how the services are connected?
koan17.q1OptCorrect: Yes, each trace shows which services called which
koan17.q1OptWrong2: Only if you read the source code
koan17.q1Correct: Exactly. Every trace is a record of which service called which. Let’s prove it.
koan17.q1Wrong: Think about what a trace records: it captures the path a request takes through services.
koan17.hintClickTrace1: Click Trace 1 to begin building the map.
koan17.predictionNewEdge:
Trace {n} shows: {desc}
Which new connection will appear on the map?
koan17.predictionNoNewEdge:
Trace {n} shows: {desc}
All these connections are already on the map. What will change?
koan17.predCorrectNoNew: Right. Repeated traces thicken existing edges, showing call frequency.
koan17.predCorrectNew: Correct! You predicted the new connection.
koan17.predWrong: Look at the trace chain carefully. Which pair of services isn’t on the map yet?
koan17.q2: Look at the map you just built. Which service receives the most incoming calls?
koan17.q2Correct: Every trace passes through api. It receives 5 incoming calls. You can see it in the map you built.
koan17.q2Wrong: Count the arrows pointing into each service on the map you built.
koan17.insightText: No one drew the map. The traces drew it. Every span is a connection between services.

koan18.title: Correlation
koan18.zen:
One signal sees the shadow. Another finds the shape. A third reveals the cause.
Apart, they are clues. Together, they are the answer.
koan18.narrative1: Something is slow. The metric tells you THAT something is wrong, but not WHY.
koan18.q1: Which signal would help you find the specific slow request?
koan18.q1FbMetric: Metrics show aggregates: averages, percentiles, counts. They can’t show you an individual request.
koan18.q1FbTrace: Traces follow individual requests through the system. They can show you exactly which request was slow and where.
koan18.q1FbResource: Resources identify where telemetry comes from, but they don’t follow individual requests.
koan18.narrative2: The trace shows WHERE the time was spent, almost all of it in the database call. But WHY is the database slow?
koan18.q2: Which signal would tell you what happened inside the database?
koan18.q2FbMetric: Metrics show trends over time, not what happened in a specific request.
koan18.q2FbLog: Logs capture discrete events with detail: the specific error, the specific cause.
koan18.q2FbSpanAttr: Span attributes add context, but a log record captures the full event with its own detail.
koan18.insightLine1: Metrics tell you something is wrong. Traces show you where. Logs tell you why.
koan18.insightLine2: Correlation is the thread that connects them.

koan19.pageTitle: Koan 19: The Full Picture - OpenTelemetry Koans
koan19.title: The Full Picture
koan19.zen:
The system failed on a Tuesday.
But this time, the developer did not ask “When did this begin?”
They opened three windows and found the answer themselves.
koan19.narr0:
An alert fires. Something is wrong. You have three tools at your disposal.
Where do you start?
koan19.chooserHint1: There is no wrong answer. Each reveals a different piece.
koan19.metricFinding:
resource.service.name = orders
error_rate = 12.4% · p99_latency = 3,200ms · window = 14:30 – 14:40 UTC
koan19.metricPartialClue: You know THAT something is wrong with orders, and WHEN it started. But not WHAT is failing or WHY.
koan19.tracePanelHeader: trace search - orders service - errors only
koan19.traceIdLine: trace_id: f7a2c9e1...3b8d · root: gateway · error at: database span
koan19.tracePartialClue: You can see WHERE the error happens, the database span. But the trace doesn’t say WHY the database call failed.
koan19.logPartialClue: Errors are scattered across six services. Without knowing the request path, it is hard to tell which error is the cause and which is a symptom.
koan19.narr1:
One signal gave you a piece. Not the whole answer.
What do you check next?
koan19.chooserHint2: You need at least two signals to narrow down the problem.
koan19.narr2:
Two signals, two perspectives. One showed the shape, the other added context.
The last signal will complete the picture.
koan19.narr3:
You have seen all three signals. Errors appear in gateway, api, and orders. Logs mention payment and inventory too.
But which service is the actual root cause?
koan19.serviceMapPrompt: Click the service where the failure originates.
koan19.serviceMapHint: Think about what the trace showed you.
koan19.serviceMapCorrect: Correct. The database is the origin. Everything upstream failed because of it.
koan19.serviceMapWrongGateway: Gateway returned 502, but only because it never got a response. Look deeper.
koan19.serviceMapWrongApi: The api forwarded the request. The error originated further downstream.
koan19.serviceMapWrongOrders: Close. Orders failed, but the trace showed the error in the span below orders.
koan19.serviceMapWrongPayment: Payment had warnings, but the primary error path goes through orders, not payment.
koan19.serviceMapWrongInventory: Inventory was slow, but the failing trace never even reached inventory.
koan19.serviceMapWrongFallback: Look at the trace waterfall again.
koan19.rootCauseText:
The database connection pool is exhausted.
Every service above it in the call chain, orders, api, gateway, fails as a consequence. The log warnings from payment and inventory are symptoms of the same bottleneck.
koan19.completionLine1: You have walked the path of 19 koans.
koan19.completionLine2: From a silent system to one that speaks in metrics, traces, and logs.
